ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'tonic water' class (FOODON_03301099); no specific 'flavoured tonic water' subclass exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers tonic water generically; no flavoured-variant subcode exists in the published hierarchy. Closest parent match applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for flavoured tonic water identified in the clinical terminology hierarchy; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard single-serve can of flavoured tonic water is approximately 330 ml (330 g), providing about 40 kcal and 9.7 g of carbohydrate per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Flavoured Tonic Water — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id D06020017 is sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database with confidence 0.95 and review_status 'reviewed'. Values are accepted as-is. USDA FoodData Central entry for tonic water (FDC ID 174831) was cross-checked and is consistent with the HPB figures for energy, carbohydrate, and sodium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for flavoured tonic water exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 43 is estimated from the closest analogous approved-source entry: regular carbonated soft drink / sugar-sweetened carbonated beverage (GI ~40–65 range; cola-type beverages GI ~63, lemonade ~54, tonic water analogues ~40–45). Given the low sugar content (2.85 g/100 g) and the presence of quinine which may modestly slow gastric emptying, a conservative estimate of GI 43 (low category) is applied. Glycemic load per 330 g serving = (43 × 9.7 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 4.2 g. This is an analogous estimate; a direct measurement is recommended for clinical precision.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar with negligible fibre and potassium; regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is associated with weight gain and elevated blood pressure
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid flavoured tonic water due to its added sugar content (2.85 g/100 g) and absence of fibre or potassium; plain sparkling water or sugar-free tonic water are lower-risk alternatives, and the beverage should never be combined with alcohol (e.g. gin) as this compounds cardiovascular risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with added sugar and zero dietary fibre, promoting rapid glucose absorption and habitual SSB intake linked to insulin resistance
type2_diabetes.guidance: Flavoured tonic water is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage and is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; even at 2.85 g sugar/100 g, the absence of fibre means rapid glucose absorption, and habitual SSB consumption independently impairs insulin sensitivity — sugar-free tonic water or plain sparkling water should be substituted.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Added sugar in a liquid form with no protective nutrients (no fibre, no potassium, no antioxidants); regular SSB intake is associated with increased CVD risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should minimise consumption of flavoured tonic water as a sugar-sweetened beverage; it provides empty calories with no cardioprotective nutrients, and its frequent use as a mixer with spirits (alcohol) further elevates cardiovascular risk — plain sparkling water is the preferred substitute.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: No anti-inflammatory nutrients present; added sugar may promote low-grade systemic inflammation; quinine may interact with certain RA medications
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Flavoured tonic water is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis as it contains no anti-inflammatory factors (no omega-3, polyphenols, antioxidants, fibre, calcium, vitamin D, or fermented cultures), added sugar may promote systemic inflammation, and quinine in tonic water may interact with some RA medications — consult a prescriber before regular consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Flavoured tonic water is a carbonated soft drink made from carbonated water, quinine (a bitter alkaloid historically derived from cinchona bark), added sugar, and artificial or natural flavourings. It is a variant of classic tonic water, distinguished by the addition of fruit or botanical flavours such as elderflower, citrus, or berry. The beverage is clear to lightly coloured, effervescent, and characterised by a mildly bitter-sweet taste profile derived from the quinine content. Per 100 g, it provides approximately 12 kcal, 2.94 g carbohydrate (of which 2.85 g is sugar), negligible protein and fat, and 5 mg sodium. Dietary fibre is absent. It is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) and is ultra-processed. Clinically, it is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its SSB classification and rapid glucose absorption profile in the absence of fibre. It is of moderate concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to added sugar and the absence of cardioprotective nutrients. Quinine may interact with certain medications including anticoagulants and some rheumatoid arthritis drugs. Flavoured tonic water is widely consumed as a mixer in cocktails, most commonly with gin. It is available globally and is popular in Singapore, the United Kingdom, and across Europe. Sugar-free variants exist and are preferable for individuals managing blood glucose or weight.
common_user_queries: How many calories in tonic water?
Is tonic water a sugar-sweetened beverage?
What is the difference between tonic water and sparkling water?
Is tonic water suitable for hypertension?
Can I drink tonic water if I have rheumatoid arthritis?
What is the glycemic index of tonic water?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D06020017, review_status 'reviewed', confidence 0.95). Cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central tonic water entry (FDC ID 174831) with consistent results. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework and is clearly documented as such.
notes: Zero-macro justification: protein_g is confirmed zero — flavoured tonic water contains no protein-bearing ingredients (water, quinine, sugar, flavourings only). fat_g is confirmed zero — no fat-containing ingredients are present in this formulation; confirmed zero per HPB Foodo database and consistent with USDA FoodData Central tonic water entry. saturated_fat_g and cholesterol_mg are confirmed zero by the same rationale. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero — no fibre-bearing ingredients present. Negative sentinel values: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 because detailed micronutrient and fatty acid breakdown for flavoured tonic water is not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this item, and the USDA FoodData Central tonic water entry (FDC ID 174831) does not report these values either. Potassium is estimated at approximately 1 mg/100 g in some sources but is not confirmed in the HPB record and is therefore set to -1. GI is an analogous estimate (GI 43, low category) based on the University of Sydney GI Database framework for sugar-sweetened carbonated beverages; no direct measurement for flavoured tonic water exists. The 'keto' dietary tag from the input has been removed as flavoured tonic water contains added sugar (2.85 g/100 g) and is not consistent with a ketogenic dietary pattern. Ontology codes use closest_parent match designations as no exact flavoured-tonic-water subclass exists in FoodOn or FoodEx2 as of 2026.
